cards.back: Whitespace refers to characters used for formatting purposes: spaces, tabs, and newlines. The compiler generally ignores whitespace (with some exceptions).

cards.text: Some language elements must be {{c1::whitespace-separated}}, such as when two keywords or identifiers are placed next to each other (e.g., {{c2::int main}}).

cards.question: Are these two code snippets equivalent?
cards.explanation: The compiler treats them the same way because whitespace (beyond required separation) is ignored. However, Version 2 is much more readable and is the preferred style.

Proper formatting is critical for code readability and maintainability:
  • Makes code structure visually clear
  • Shows relationships between code blocks
  • Reduces errors by making mistakes more obvious
  • Helps other developers (and future you) understand the code
  • Professional code is always well-formatted
examples:
// Bad: hard to read
int main(){std::cout<<"Hi";return 0;}

cards.text: Each statement within curly braces should start one tab (or {{c1::4 spaces}}) in from the opening brace. This is called {{c2::indentation}}.

cards.explanation:
There are different styles for placing curly braces:

Allman style (preferred for beginners):
Opening brace on its own line

K&R style:
Opening brace on same line as statement

Both are acceptable. Choose one and be consistent throughout your codebase.

cards.front: Does whitespace inside quoted text (strings) get ignored by the compiler?
cards.back: No. Whitespace inside quoted text is not ignored. "Hello World" is different from "HelloWorld".

cards.front: Should you use tabs or spaces for indentation?
cards.back: Either is acceptable, but spaces are generally preferred (often 4 spaces per indentation level). Most importantly, be consistent within a project. Modern editors can convert tabs to spaces automatically.

cards.text: It's conventional to put spaces around binary operators: {{c1::x = 2 + 3;}} rather than {{c2::x=2+3;}}

cards.explanation:
Use automatic code formatters to maintain consistent style:
  • Eliminates debates about formatting style
  • Ensures consistency across the codebase
  • Saves time - no manual formatting needed
  • Common tools: clang-format, VS Code formatters
  • Can enforce team/project style guidelines
examples: Can run on save or as pre-commit hook
